summary: A hyperlink — the element the web is named after.
use: For navigation to a URL or fragment, with link text that predicts the destination; use rel to describe the relationship (external, license, author, nofollow).
avoid: Never 'click here', never href='#' as a fake button (that's button), and never a link that does nothing.

summary: Stress emphasis that changes the meaning of the sentence.
use: Where a speaker would stress the word: 'I never said that' differs from 'I never said that'.
avoid: Not for italics-as-style (that's i or CSS) and not for importance (that's strong).
snippet: Do not add more flour — the dough is supposed to be sticky.

summary: Strong importance, seriousness, or urgency.
use: For warnings and key facts the reader must not miss; nesting increases importance.
avoid: Not for bold-as-style — attention without extra importance is b.
snippet: Warning: the pan will be extremely hot.

summary: Side comments and small print: copyright lines, disclaimers, legal caveats.
use: For fine print that qualifies the main content.
avoid: Not for de-emphasizing whole blocks or just making text smaller — that's CSS.

summary: Content that is no longer accurate or relevant, struck through but kept visible.
use: Old prices, sold-out notices, superseded facts that weren't formal 'edits'.
avoid: For tracked document edits use del; for decoration use CSS text-decoration.

summary: The title of a creative work: book, article, film, specification, song.
use: Around work titles wherever they're referenced, including blockquote attributions.
avoid: Not for people's names — the spec is explicit that cite marks the work, not the author.
snippet: See HTML: The Living Standard for the definitive rules.

summary: A short inline quotation with browser-supplied, locale-correct quotation marks.
use: For quotes inside a sentence; the marks adapt to the lang attribute (compare German „…“ with English “…”).
avoid: Don't type the quote characters yourself around a q, and use blockquote for block-level quotes.
snippet: The spec calls a section a thematic grouping of content.

summary: The defining instance of a term — the one place where the term is explained.
use: On a term's first, defining mention; give it an id so other pages and passages can link to the definition.
avoid: Only the defining occurrence gets dfn — later mentions are plain text or links back to it.
attributes: title — the term being defined, if the content isn't it
snippet: A landmark is a region screen readers can jump to directly.

summary: An abbreviation or acronym, optionally with its expansion in title.
use: Spell the term out on first use, then abbr for the rest; the title shows on hover and is available to assistive tech.
avoid: Don't rely on title alone to convey essential meaning — touch users never see tooltips.
snippet: The W3C publishes accessibility guidance.

summary: Annotates base text with small readings above it — furigana, pinyin, pronunciation guides.
use: For East Asian pronunciation annotations; pair with rt for the reading and rp for fallback parentheses.
avoid: Not for tooltips or footnotes in Latin-script prose — it's a typographic convention, not a comment mechanism.

summary: The ruby text — the small annotation rendered above (or beside) the base characters.
use: Inside ruby, one rt per annotated run of base text.
avoid: Only valid inside ruby.

summary: Fallback parentheses shown only by browsers that can't render ruby layout.
use: Wrap the rt in a pair of rp elements containing '(' and ')' so the annotation degrades to '漢(kan)'.
avoid: Only valid inside ruby, flanking rt.

summary: Pairs human-readable content with a machine-readable value.
use: For SKUs, quantities, scores, IDs — anything a script or crawler should read precisely while humans read prose.
avoid: For dates, times, and durations use time instead — it's the specialized form.

summary: A date, time, or duration with a machine-readable datetime attribute.
use: For every date and duration on a page — search engines read the datetime (2026-07-24, PT45M) while humans read the prose.
avoid: Don't skip the datetime when the content isn't already machine-parseable, and don't use it for vague times ('the olden days').
attributes: datetime — ISO date, time, or duration

summary: A fragment of computer code: an element name, a function, a filename, a command.
use: Inline for code tokens in prose; wrap in pre for block listings.
avoid: Not for keyboard input (kbd), program output (samp), or placeholders (var) — each has its own element.

summary: A variable or placeholder the reader is expected to substitute.
use: In command synopses and math: 'replace <var>file</var> with your output path'.
avoid: Not for italics — its meaning is 'this is a placeholder', nothing else.

summary: Sample output from a program or system.
use: For terminal output, error messages, and log lines — everything the computer said back.
avoid: Not for code you type (code) or keys you press (kbd).
snippet: The build prints 3 files converted, 0 errors on success.

summary: User input — keystrokes, voice commands, menu selections.
use: Nest one kbd per physical key inside an outer kbd for the whole chord: Ctrl+S becomes three elements.
avoid: Not for code fragments — kbd is what the user does, code is what the program is.

summary: Subscript text with typographic meaning.
use: Chemistry (H₂O), math indices, footnote conventions that require subscripts.
avoid: Not for styling small lowered text — only where subscript position is part of the notation.

summary: Superscript text with typographic meaning.
use: Exponents (x²), ordinals in some languages, footnote markers.
avoid: Not for styling raised text — only notation.

summary: Text in an alternate voice or mood: taxonomy, ship names, foreign phrases, technical terms.
use: Where convention italicizes without stress: déjà vu — and add lang for foreign phrases.
avoid: If the meaning changes when read with stress, that's em, not i.
attributes: lang — language of a foreign phrase

summary: Text drawn to the reader's attention without extra importance: keywords, product names, article leads.
use: For visual salience with no urgency — keywords in a summary, names in a review.
avoid: If it's genuinely important or urgent, that's strong.
snippet: The winning entry was a sourdough rye from Rotterdam.

summary: A non-textual annotation — the rare legitimate underline: misspelling marks, Chinese proper-name marks.
use: Almost never; its one idiomatic modern use is the proper-name mark in Chinese text.
avoid: Never underline links' lookalikes with it, and never use it for emphasis — readers assume underlines are links.

summary: Text highlighted for contextual relevance — a search hit, a referenced passage.
use: To mark why this passage matters right now: matched search terms, the line under discussion.
avoid: Not decoration and not emphasis — the highlight must answer 'relevant to what?'.
snippet: …save the page for offline reading; there are no stylesheets…

summary: Bidirectional isolation: renders text of unknown direction without disturbing its surroundings.
use: Around user-generated names and titles in lists — without it, an RTL name can visually swallow the number next to it.
avoid: Not needed for text whose direction you already control with dir on a container.

summary: Bidirectional override: forces a run of text to render in an explicit direction.
use: Rarely — only when you must override the Unicode bidi algorithm deliberately.
avoid: For isolating unknown-direction text use bdi; for whole blocks use the dir attribute.

summary: The generic inline container with no meaning — the inline counterpart of div.
use: Only when no semantic inline element fits and you need a hook for lang, translate, id, or microdata.
avoid: A span without an attribute justifying it is a defect; check for a real element first.
attributes: lang / translate / id — the usual justifications
snippet: The imprint Field Guide keeps its name in every locale.

summary: A line break that is part of the content itself.
use: Poems, postal addresses, song lyrics — where the break carries meaning.
avoid: Never for spacing between blocks; that's margins in CSS.

summary: A word-break opportunity inside an otherwise unbreakable token.
use: In long URLs, file paths, and identifiers so they can wrap at sensible points.
avoid: Not a substitute for CSS overflow-wrap on general prose.
